stimulus:
The following text is from Ina Coolbrith’s 1895 poem “The Mountain’s Word.”

No matter how we terrace up the slope,

 Hedging the meadow and the orchard close;

No matter how we channel down the rain,

Bidding the dry hill flower at our hand,

 We must always leave the shoulders of the peak;

 The unmeasured reaches

 Of the windy snow-swept beaches,

The lonely upper margin of the peak.
stem: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?

answerOptions.content: The speaker argues against interfering with nature and then gives evidence supporting this interference.
The speaker presents an account of efforts to dominate nature and then cautions that such efforts are only temporary.
The speaker describes attempts to control nature and then offers a reminder that not all nature is controllable.
The speaker provides examples of an admirable way of approaching nature and then challenges that approach.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer. In the first half of the text, the speaker describes attempts to control nature: terracing slopes, hedging meadows and orchards, channeling rainwater, and bidding dry hill flowers to bloom. In the second half, the speaker states that we must always leave the “shoulders of the peak” and its “lonely upper margin”—reminding us that not all nature is controllable.

Choice A is incorrect because the speaker does not argue against interfering with nature; the speaker simply notes a limit. Choice B is incorrect because the speaker does not describe these efforts as temporary. Choice D is incorrect because the speaker does not describe these efforts as admirable.
